title: Ozel Marches as Court Crisis Deepens, Fidan Courts Tokyo on Drones
subhead: Tens of thousands rally for ousted CHP leader Ozgur Ozel in Ankara as Turkey's opposition crisis escalates, while Foreign Minister Fidan signals drone cooperation with Japan and a US-Iran deal appears imminent with major implications for Ankara's regional position.

sections.heading: Ousted CHP Leader Ozel Draws Massive Rally in Ankara
sections.body:
Tens of thousands of supporters of Ozgur Ozel marched through central Ankara on Saturday, in the largest show of opposition force since a court ruling removed him as leader of the Republican People's Party (CHP) on May 21. Crowds gathered in Guven Park to hear Ozel condemn his removal before marching to Ataturk's mausoleum. 'They are attempting to replace the CHP's elected chairman and appoint a trustee,' Ozel told supporters. 'Today is the day to restart our march to power.'

The appeals court ruling overturned the 2023 party congress that elected Ozel, reinstating his predecessor Kemal Kilicdaroglu. On Saturday, Kilicdaroglu held a rival gathering at CHP headquarters — the same building riot police stormed on May 24 to remove Ozel and his supporters — and promised to bring a ballot box for a new party congress 'as soon as possible.' Ozel challenged Kilicdaroglu to an immediate primary, saying he would step aside if he received less than 85% support. Crowds chanted 'Traitor Kemal!' as the rivalry between the two CHP factions turned personal.

According to RFI, Turkey expert Gonul Tol of the Middle East Institute described Kilicdaroglu as 'a convenient opponent for Erdogan, because he lost every major election during his 13 years at the helm of the party.' The Supreme Election Council, which certified Ozel's 2023 win, has constitutional authority over party elections — raising questions about the court's jurisdiction to override it.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP crisis is the most serious institutional confrontation between Erdogan and the opposition since Imamoglu's arrest in March 2025. A court overriding the election council to remove an opposition leader is without precedent in modern Turkey and signals that judicial pressure on the opposition is escalating, not abating.

sections.heading: Erdogan Capitalizes on Distracted West, Tightens Grip at Home
sections.body:
A Telegraph analysis published Saturday argues that Erdogan is the real winner of the Iran war — not because of military gains, but because the global preoccupation with the Hormuz crisis has given him room to accelerate an anti-democratic backslide with no repercussions. Gonul Tol of the Middle East Institute told the Telegraph: 'All those geopolitical shocks are allowing and emboldening Erdogan to do whatever he wants with whatever is left of the democratic space at home.'

Nine days after the Iran war began in February, Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu — Erdogan's most formidable rival — went on trial facing charges that could result in a combined sentence exceeding 2,000 years. He has been imprisoned since March 2025. The Telegraph notes that not a single Western government has publicly criticized the prosecution. Even Britain's Labour Party, the sister party of the CHP, has been muted.

Political commentator Sezin Oney of Politikyol told RFI that Erdogan's broader aim is to 'design the succession, most probably to have a close family member take over.' The CHP is currently level with the ruling AKP in most recent opinion polls, and the next election is not due until 2028 — though many expect Erdogan to push for early elections while the opposition is in disarray.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has created a permissive environment for democratic backsliding that Erdogan is methodically exploiting. The pattern — crisis abroad, crackdown at home — is consistent with his playbook after the 2013 Gezi protests and the 2016 coup attempt. The difference now is that Western capitals feel they need Erdogan's cooperation too urgently to push back.

sections.heading: Fidan Signals Drone Cooperation With Japan, Says Iran Deal 'Closer Than Ever'
sections.body:
In an exclusive interview with Nikkei Asia published Saturday, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said Turkey is eager to cooperate with Japan on unmanned aerial systems, including joint development and co-production of drones. Fidan framed the push as part of a broader middle-power cooperation strategy, telling Nikkei that countries like Turkey and Japan should deepen partnerships as the traditional US-led security architecture fragments.

Fidan also said a US-Iran deal to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz appeared 'closer than ever.' His assessment aligns with reporting from AP that US and Iranian negotiators reached a tentative agreement on Thursday to extend the ceasefire by 60 days and begin new talks on Iran's nuclear program. Vice President JD Vance confirmed a tentative agreement exists but said it was unclear whether Trump would approve it. Iran's foreign ministry separately stated Friday that 'no negotiations' were taking place on the nuclear issue — a contradictory signal typical of the diplomatic fog surrounding the talks.
sections.whyItMatters: The Japan drone push reflects Ankara's effort to position Turkey as a defense-industrial partner for advanced economies, not just a buyer. If Turkey and Japan jointly develop drones, it would mark a significant upgrade in Turkey's defense technology partnerships beyond its current base of Gulf, African, and Central Asian clients. Fidan's Iran deal assessment, if accurate, means Ankara will soon need to adjust its regional calculus for a post-war Persian Gulf.

sections.heading: South Caucasus Energy Routes Elevate Turkey's Transit Role
sections.body:
A Forbes analysis published Saturday examines the intensifying competition over South Caucasus energy and trade routes, with Turkey as a central player. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio visited Yerevan on May 26 to sign a strategic cooperation agreement with Armenia covering energy, following Vice President JD Vance's February visit that produced a civil nuclear energy cooperation deal. The Forbes piece notes that both the US and China benefit from normalized Armenia-Azerbaijan relations and functioning transit networks through the Caucasus — while Russia and Iran benefit from the opposite.

Turkey's existing energy infrastructure, including the TANAP pipeline and the broader Southern Gas Corridor, gives it a decisive transit role for European energy diversification. The Middle Corridor trade route — linking China to Europe via the Caucasus and Turkey — depends on regional stability that Ankara can help broker or withhold. Iran views the Azerbaijan-Turkey-Israel axis with hostility and fears transport corridors that could bypass it.
sections.whyItMatters: The South Caucasus competition reinforces Turkey's value as an energy and trade transit hub precisely when the Hormuz crisis has made alternative routes more critical. Armenia's June 7 elections will determine whether normalization proceeds, and Turkey's position as Azerbaijan's closest ally gives it leverage over the outcome.

sections.heading: PKK Peace Process Remains Stalled as War Rattles the Region
sections.body:
The Iran war has brought Turkey's peace process with the PKK to a near standstill, according to a Reuters/Al-Monitor analysis from mid-May that remains the most recent authoritative assessment. Both sides have dug in: the government insists on full disarmament before legislative reforms, while the PKK — which announced its dissolution last year — says doing so without legal guarantees would leave it exposed while 'drones and missiles are flying overhead,' in the words of senior PKK officer Murat Karayilan.

Pro-Kurdish DEM Party lawmaker Gulistan Kilic Kocyigit told Reuters the process has paused but not completely halted. She believes the government is 'to some extent, waiting for developments in Iran and the broader Middle East.' PKK political wing spokesperson Zagros Hiwa said Turkey has 'unilaterally frozen' the process to boost the ruling party's political prospects. Erdogan's nationalist ally MHP leader Devlet Bahceli recently suggested jailed PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan should be granted an official role — the government has not responded.

A Konda Barometer survey in December found 79% of respondents believed the state was wrong to engage with Ocalan, including 62% of AKP voters — indicating that public support for the peace process, even among Erdogan's base, has eroded significantly.
sections.whyItMatters: The longer the PKK process stays frozen, the harder it becomes to restart. If the Iran war produces a settlement that reshapes power dynamics in northern Iraq, the PKK's operating environment could change dramatically — but so far, the war has only made both sides more risk-averse.

sections.heading: Defense Industry Surge: Canada, Japan, and Multibillion-Dollar Deals
sections.body:
Turkey's defense industry momentum continues to build on multiple fronts. At SAHA Expo 2026 in Istanbul earlier this month, Canada's Secretary of State for Defense Procurement Stephen Fuhr described Turkey as a 'trusted partner' and 'valuable ally,' telling Defense News that 'the middle powers have to come together in a way they didn't before.' Fuhr identified ammunition production, drones, and counter-drone systems as priority areas for cooperation, and said Ottawa is focused on co-development rather than off-the-shelf purchases.

The visit marked a striking reversal: Canada imposed a de facto arms embargo on Turkey from 2019 to January 2024. Fuhr — elected in 2025 — said both sides prioritized 'responding to the moment' over revisiting past disputes. He compared Turkey's indigenous defense industrial base to those of South Korea and France. Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is planning a visit to Turkey, and a free trade agreement is under discussion.

Meanwhile, Turkish defense firm Roketsan is targeting $1 billion in exports as it expands AI-powered weapons systems, according to Anadolu Agency. Sefine Shipyard's SIHAM research center signed an MOU with Canada-based Kraken Robotics during SAHA. Turkey is also procuring 100 one-way explosive naval drones for swarm attack capabilities.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is transitioning from a defense exporter serving Gulf and African markets to a co-development partner for advanced economies like Canada and potentially Japan. The speed at which former adversaries are seeking defense partnerships with Ankara reflects both Turkey's growing industrial capabilities and the vacuum created by US retrenchment.

bottomLine: Saturday's developments crystallize Turkey's dual-track reality: at home, the judicial removal of CHP leader Ozgur Ozel drew the largest opposition rally since the crisis began, deepening a constitutional confrontation that Erdogan is exploiting while the world looks elsewhere; abroad, Foreign Minister Fidan is actively courting Japan for drone co-development and signaling that a US-Iran deal is near — a development that would reshape Turkey's regional calculations from Hormuz energy flows to the stalled PKK peace process. Turkey's defense industry continues its rapid ascent, with Canada pivoting from a recent arms embargo to seeking co-development partnerships. The pattern is consistent: Ankara gains leverage externally while democratic space contracts internally, and the Iran war has amplified both dynamics simultaneously.
sources: Asharq Al-Awsat, Nikkei Asia, AP, RFI, Al-Monitor/Reuters, Telegraph/Yahoo News, Forbes, Defense News, Anadolu Agency, CNBC, Middle East Institute
